evidence_excerpt: After diagnosis in the neonatal period, macular pigmentary changes at 5 months progressed to bilateral, atrophic macular lesions by 10 months; long-term visual acuity remained poor (20/250 and 20/400).

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Persistent severe central vision loss maintained since age 5 and unchanged pendular nystagmus.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: At age 5 months macular pigmentary changes were noted in both eyes.
By 10 months macular pigmentary changes had progressed to bilateral, atrophic macular lesions.
At the same time there was developmental regression and hypotonia.
At 2 years, large angle esotropia was treated with bilateral medial rectus resections.
He later developed a consecutive exotropia.
By age 13, best corrected visual acuity was 20/250 (RE) and 20/400 (LE), a level maintained since it was first measurable at age 5 years.
He was well rehabilitated by a low vision service; in school he functions well with the assistance of an iPad and ambulates without assistance.

notes_on_uncertainty: Index time was proxied to the neonatal diagnosis date. Long-term functional status and symptom burden are inferred from clinical descriptions at age 13; precise dates for some events are approximate within reported ages.
